message.content:
You are a web research agent. Research the GENERAL techniques that distinguish top deep-research agents, and the frontier baselines (OpenAI Deep Research, Google Gemini Deep Research, Perplexity, Anthropic). Use WebSearch and WebFetch (load them via ToolSearch with query "select:WebSearch,WebFetch" first).

Find and report CONCRETELY with source URLs:

1. OpenAI Deep Research: architecture (o3-based, end-to-end RL trained on browsing tasks), how many sources, test-time compute. Its RACE score on DeepResearch Bench if reported. OpenAI's own claims about training it with RL on real browsing tasks.

2. Google Gemini Deep Research: architecture — Google published details of a "planner" that creates a multi-step research plan the user approves, asynchronous execution, iterative "reason-search" loop, Gemini's long context (1M) for holding many sources, "reciprocal" / RETRO-style synthesis. Get the concrete planning + iterative retrieval design and its RACE score.

3. Anthropic multi-agent research system: Anthropic published an engineering blog "How we built our multi-agent research system" — a LEAD agent (orchestrator) that spawns parallel SUBAGENTS, each doing independent search, then synthesizes. Get the concrete design: orchestrator-worker pattern, parallel subagents, why multi-agent beats single-agent, token usage (they say multi-agent uses ~15x tokens), how they decide breadth/depth. This is highly relevant.

4. Academic/general findings on what makes deep-research reports score well: papers on "test-time scaling for research agents", "agentic RL for search" (Search-R1, R1-Searcher, WebThinker, DeepResearcher), the role of ITERATIVE retrieval + reflection, and cross-source SYNTHESIS vs summarization. Any paper that ties specific techniques to Comprehensiveness / Insight / analytical depth in long reports.

5. Specifically: what techniques are repeatedly cited as driving INSIGHT and COMPREHENSIVENESS in long research reports? (e.g. iterative deepening, more sources, planning/outline-first, cross-source contradiction resolution, reflection/gap-analysis loops, larger generator models, RL for report writing).

Report exact numbers, model names, method names, and source URLs. Mark unknown where unpublished. Do not guess.

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- artifact-design: Design guidance and fundamentals for Artifacts.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).

Gemini-2.5-Pro Deep Research demonstrated leading overall performance (48.88) across all dimensions, with OpenAI Deep Research following. More specifically, Gemini currently leads DeepResearch Bench with a 48.9 score, while OpenAI, Claude, and Kimi trail by less than four points.

Based on the search results, here's what I found about Anthropic's multi-agent research system:

## Architecture Overview

Claude's Research is an orchestrator-worker system: a lead agent plans, spins up 3-5 specialized subagents in parallel, and synthesizes their findings with a separate citation pass.

### Key Components

**Lead Agent:** When a user submits a query, the Lead Researcher analyzes it, decides on an overall strategy, and records the plan in memory. The lead agent maintains overall research state through a memory system that persists context when conversations exceed 200,000 tokens, preventing loss of research plans and findings.

**Subagents:** These are specialized agents created by the Lead Researcher. Each subagent is given a specific task, such as exploring a certain company, checking a particular time period, or looking into a technical detail. Each subagent gets its own context window, enabling true parallelization.

## Performance and Token Usage

On their internal eval, this setup beat single-agent Claude Opus 4 by 90.2%. However, the cost: roughly 15x the tokens of a normal chat.

**What Drives Performance:** Three factors explained 95% of performance variance: Token Usage 80%, Number of Tool Calls ~10%, Model Choice ~5%.

## Use Cases

The economics only work for high-value research: legal due diligence, competitive intelligence, biomedical literature review.

Based on my web search results, I've found comprehensive information about Google Gemini Deep Research and the RACE score. Here's what I found:

## Google Gemini Deep Research

Google DeepMind recently introduced Gemini DR, an advanced deep research agent based on its multimodal Gemini 2.0 Flash Thinking model.

### Key Capabilities

Gemini Deep Research delivers four significant advancements: (1) Interactive Research Planning, where it autonomously formulates a multi-step investigation plan for interactive user review and modification; (2) Asynchronous Task Management, which adopts an asynchronous task management architecture to efficiently handle multiple simultaneous tasks; and (3) Large-scale context windows RAG ensembles for managing and synthesizing multimodal data.

### Multi-Step Research Planning

The Gemini Deep Research Agent autonomously plans, executes, and synthesizes multi-step research tasks. Gemini Deep Research Max is a multi-step agentic research tool that plans, searches, reads, and iterates before generating a report.

### Asynchronous and Iterative Approach

A novel asynchronous task manager was developed that maintains a shared state between the planner and task models, allowing for graceful error recovery without restarting the entire task. At each step, the model grounds itself on all information gathered so far, then identifies missing information and discrepancies that it wants to explore.

## RACE Score

RACE is an adaptive framework that generates dynamic evaluation criteria and weights for assessing long-form LLM research outputs. It employs a multi-phase methodology including dimension weighting, criterion generation, and reference scoring to benchmark candidate reports. The framework infers dimension weights that reflect the relative importance of distinct quality aspects including "Comprehensiveness," "Insight/Depth," "Instruction-Following," and "Readability."

# Anthropic's Multi-Agent Research System: Design Details

## Architecture Pattern
The system employs an **orchestrator-worker design** where a lead agent coordinates specialized subagents. The lead agent "analyzes it, develops a strategy, and spawns subagents to explore different aspects simultaneously."

## Parallel Subagent Deployment
The document specifies that the lead agent "spins up 3-5 subagents in parallel rather than serially" for complex tasks, with subagents also using "3+ tools in parallel."

## Performance Gains
The multi-agent approach dramatically outperforms single-agent systems: "multi-agent system with Claude Opus 4 as the lead agent and Claude Sonnet 4 subagents outperformed single-agent Claude Opus 4 by 90.2%." The example demonstrates this with board member identification tasks where single agents "failed to find the answer with slow, sequential searches."

## Token Economics
Token usage drives performance variance significantly: "token usage by itself explains 80% of the variance" in research quality, while "the number of tool calls and the model choice" account for other factors. Overall, "agents typically use about 4× more tokens than chat interactions, and multi-agent systems use about 15× more tokens than chats."

## Breadth vs. Depth Strategy
The system employs "Start wide, then narrow down" methodology: agents should explore landscape broadly first through "short, broad queries," then "progressively narrow focus" into specifics.

## Citation Processing
After research completion, "the system exits the research loop and passes all findings to a CitationAgent, which processes the documents and research report to identify specific locations for citations."

Based on my search results, I found information about several of the systems and research areas you mentioned. Here's what I discovered:

## Key Agentic Deep Research Systems

R1-Searcher-7B is an open-weight agent that enables LLMs to invoke external search systems via a two-stage outcome-supervised RL framework, while Search-R1-32B is an open-weight agent that interleaves reasoning and retrieval through RL.

Search-R1, built upon veRL, extends the ideas of DeepSeek-R1(-Zero) by incorporating interleaved search engine access and provides a fully open-source RL training pipeline, serving as an alternative and open solution to OpenAI DeepResearch.

Recent RL-based agentic deep research works include Search-R1, R1-Searcher, DeepResearcher, WebThinker, and other systems that have extended outcome-supervised reinforcement learning to agentic deep research.

## How These Systems Work

These systems use a multi-turn loop in which the LLM acts as an RL agent alternating between producing reasoning and search query tokens, with reasoning and retrieval co-optimized through an embedded environment rather than treated as separate sequential steps.

Recent RL-based systems such as Search-R1, R1-Searcher, DeepResearcher, ZeroSearch, and WebAgent-R1 operate in various search environments from static local corpora and open search APIs to real-world web interfaces.

Based on the search results, here's what I found about the DeepResearch Bench paper and the RACE framework:

## DeepResearch Bench and RACE Framework Overview

DeepResearch Bench is the first specialized benchmark for evaluating Deep Research Agents, comprising 100 PhD-level research tasks meticulously crafted by domain experts across 22 distinct fields.

### RACE Framework Components

RACE (Reference-based Adaptive Criteria-driven Evaluation with Dynamic Weighting) is an automated framework designed to deliver human-aligned and task-sensitive evaluation of long-form research outputs generated by LLM-based research agents.

**Evaluation Dimensions:** The framework uses Gemini-2.5-pro to assess report quality based on four dimensions: Comprehensiveness, Insight/Depth, Instruction-Following, and Readability.

**Key Operational Stages:** The framework operates in three key stages: dynamic weight and adaptive criteria generation, reference-based scoring, and overall score calculation.

**How it Works:** An LLM acting as a meta-evaluator analyzes the input query to determine the relative importance of evaluation dimensions, while a separate LLM then scores the target report against criteria derived for each dimension.

**Addressing Bias:** By comparing target reports with high-quality references, RACE mitigates biases and ensures a more accurate evaluation of report quality.

### Performance Results

Gemini-2.5-Pro Deep Research demonstrates leading overall performance on the RACE framework, with experiments revealing a strong linear correlation between RACE scores and human judgments, suggesting the framework effectively captures meaningful performance differences between models.

content.content:
# Key Techniques Driving Deep Research Agent Quality

## Core Findings from the Paper

The document identifies three primary technical approaches that enhance deep research quality:

**1. Iterative Retrieval Over Single Retrieval**
The paper emphasizes that "a single search step often falls short of capturing comprehensive information, while isolated reasoning phases can fail to identify critical insights." Multi-hop retrieval enables "iterative, sequential searches and reasoning steps across multiple data sources," progressively building comprehensive context.

**2. Reasoning-Search Integration Loop**
Rather than discrete stages, the paradigm treats "retrieval and reasoning as interdependent, continuously co-evolving." This dynamic feedback loop allows "reasoning actively influences search (e.g., refining search queries based on intermediate deductions), while retrieved information recursively refines reasoning."

**3. Test-Time Scaling (TTS) with Trade-offs**
Performance improves through allocating computational resources to both reasoning depth and search breadth. The paper notes "search-heavy tasks...prioritize broader content access, while reasoning-heavy tasks...require deeper internal processing."

## Named Methods and Contributions

| Method | Key Contribution |
|--------|------------------|
| **Search-R1** | RL-optimized multi-step retrieval policies improving factual accuracy |
| **DeepResearcher** | Reinforcement learning in real-world environments for adaptive strategies |
| **WebThinker** | Combines RL with multi-modal reasoning across web sources |
| **ReSearch** | Emphasizes transparent, interpretable reasoning alongside outcome quality |

The paper demonstrates OpenAI's Deep Research achieving 51.5% on BrowseComp versus standard LLMs below 10%, validating integrated agentic approaches over traditional methods.

Based on the search results, here's information about WebThinker:

## WebThinker Overview

WebThinker is a deep research agent that empowers large reasoning models (LRMs) to autonomously search the web, navigate web pages, and draft research reports during the reasoning process.

## Key Components

WebThinker integrates a Deep Web Explorer module, enabling LRMs to dynamically search, navigate, and extract information from the web when encountering knowledge gaps. It also employs an Autonomous Think-Search-and-Draft strategy, allowing the model to seamlessly interleave reasoning, information gathering, and report writing in real time.

## Training Approach

To further enhance research tool utilization, WebThinker introduces an RL-based training strategy via iterative online Direct Preference Optimization (DPO).

## Performance Results

On GPQA, GAIA, WebWalkerQA, and HLE benchmarks, WebThinker-32B-RL achieved new state-of-the-art results among 32B models, outperforming both retrieval-augmented and proprietary systems like GPT-4o and DeepSeek-R1-671B, with gains up to +21.5% over baselines. For scientific report writing on the Glaive dataset, WebThinker outperformed Gemini 2.0 Deep Research and Grok3 DeeperSearch, scoring 8.1 in average quality metrics.

## Ablation Study Findings

The Autonomous Report Draft capability proves particularly crucial for report generation, with its removal causing the largest performance drop.

input.prompt: Extract the exact RACE scoring formula and any default/reported dimension weights for Comprehensiveness, Insight/Depth, Instruction-Following, Readability. How does reference-based scoring compute a score (the score = reference_score / (reference_score + target_score) style formula)? Give exact numbers/formula.

content.content:
# RACE Scoring Formula and Dimension Weights

## Final Scoring Formula

The reference-based scoring computes a normalized relative score as:

> "S_final(R_tgt) = S_int(R_tgt) / [S_int(R_tgt) + S_int(R_ref)], S_final ∈ [0,1]"

This expresses the target report's quality relative to the reference, where scores range from 0 to 1.

## Aggregation Structure

The integrated score combines dimension-level scores:

> "S_int(R) = Σ_d W_d S_d(R)"

Each dimension score aggregates its criteria:

> "S_d(R) = Σ_k w_d,k * s_R,c_d,k"

## Dimension Weights

The framework specifies four dimensions but **does not report fixed default weights**. Instead, weights are inferred per-task through multiple LLM trials:

> "W_d = (1/T) * Σ_j w_d^(j), Σ_d W_d = 1"

The only concrete example provided is an illustrative case study where the Judge LLM assigned: Insight (0.35), Comprehensiveness (0.30), with the remaining weights distributed among Instruction-Following and Readability—but these are task-specific, not defaults.

The framework emphasizes **adaptive weighting**, meaning dimension importance varies by research task rather than using static universal weights.

# Deep Research Agents: Frontier Baselines and General Techniques

Research findings with exact numbers, method names, and source URLs. Items that are unpublished are marked UNKNOWN.

## 1. OpenAI Deep Research

**Architecture / training (from OpenAI's own announcement):**
- Powered by "a version of the upcoming OpenAI **o3** model that's optimized for web browsing and data analysis."
- "Deep research was trained using **end-to-end reinforcement learning on hard browsing and reasoning tasks** across a range of domains, learning to plan and execute a multi-step trajectory to find the data it needs, **backtracking and reacting to real-time information** where necessary." This is OpenAI's explicit claim of RL on real browsing tasks.
- Can browse user-uploaded files, run a Python tool for plots, embed images, and **cite specific sentences/passages** from sources.

**Sources count / test-time compute:** OpenAI's marketing describes "finding, analyzing, and synthesizing **hundreds of online sources**." It "accomplishes in tens of minutes what would take a human many hours" — i.e., test-time compute scales with task difficulty, but exact token/tool-call budgets are UNKNOWN (unpublished).

**Benchmark numbers:**
- **Humanity's Last Exam: 26.6%** accuracy (new high at launch), per OpenAI.
- **BrowseComp: 51.5%** vs standard LLMs below 10% (cited in the agentic-search survey).
- **DeepResearch Bench RACE Overall: 46.98** — Comprehensiveness 46.87, Insight/Depth 45.25, **Instruction-Following 49.27 (highest of any agent)**, Readability 47.14. FACT: 40.79 effective citations, 77.96% citation accuracy.

Sources: https://openai.com/index/introducing-deep-research/ · https://deepresearch-bench.github.io/ · https://arxiv.org/html/2506.18959

## 2. Google Gemini Deep Research

**Architecture (planner + async + iterative loop):**
- **Interactive research planning**: the agent "autonomously formulates a multi-step investigation plan for interactive user review and modification." In the Gemini API this is explicit: request a plan, iterate with `previous_interaction_id`, then set `collaborative_planning=False` to approve/execute.
- **Asynchronous task management**: "a novel asynchronous task manager... maintains a shared state between the planner and task models, allowing for graceful error recovery without restarting the entire task." API-level: `background=True` + polling.
- **Iterative reason-search loop**: "At each step, the model grounds itself on all information gathered so far, then **identifies missing information and discrepancies** that it wants to explore" — i.e., a gap-analysis-driven retrieval loop.
- Grounds across Google Search, URL Context, Code Execution, MCP, File Search; accepts images/PDFs/docs.

**Model version:** Reported as based on Gemini 2.0 Flash Thinking at introduction; the "reciprocal"/RETRO-style synthesis and the specific 1M-context source-holding mechanism are **not confirmed in Google's published docs (UNKNOWN / black-box)**.

**Benchmark numbers (Gemini-2.5-Pro Deep Research — the current leader):**
- **RACE Overall: 48.88** (highest) — Comprehensiveness **48.53**, Insight/Depth **48.50**, Instruction-Following 49.18, Readability **49.44** (leads on all except IF).
- **FACT: 111.21 effective citations** (far highest — ~2.7x OpenAI's 40.79), 81.44% accuracy.

Sources: https://gemini.google/overview/deep-research/ · https://www.philschmid.de/deep-research-update · https://ai.google.dev/gemini-api/docs/interactions/deep-research · https://deepresearch-bench.github.io/

## 3. Anthropic Multi-Agent Research System (highly relevant)

From the Anthropic engineering blog "How we built our multi-agent research system":
- **Orchestrator-worker pattern**: a **lead agent** analyzes the query, develops a strategy, records the plan in memory, then **spawns 3-5 subagents in parallel** (not serial). Subagents each get their **own context window** and can call **3+ tools in parallel**.
- **Separate citation pass**: after research, the system exits the loop and passes all findings to a dedicated **CitationAgent** that maps report claims to source locations.
- **Why multi-agent wins**: their internal eval had the multi-agent system (**Claude Opus 4 lead + Claude Sonnet 4 subagents**) outperform single-agent Claude Opus 4 by **90.2%**. Parallelism beats slow sequential search on breadth-heavy tasks (e.g., "identify all board members").
- **Token economics**: **agents ≈ 4x chat tokens; multi-agent ≈ 15x chat tokens.** Performance-variance breakdown: **token usage alone explains ~80% of variance**, number of tool calls ~10%, model choice ~5% (three factors ≈ 95%).
- **Breadth/depth heuristic**: "**start wide, then narrow**" — short broad queries first, then progressively narrow. Economics only justified for high-value research (legal due diligence, competitive intel, biomedical review).

Source: https://www.anthropic.com/engineering/built-multi-agent-research-system

## 4. Academic / general findings — what makes reports score well

**RACE evaluation mechanics (DeepResearch Bench, arXiv:2506.11763):** RACE = Reference-based Adaptive Criteria-driven Evaluation with dynamic weighting; judge model is Gemini-2.5-Pro. 100 PhD-level tasks, 22 fields.
- Scoring formula: `S_final = S_int(R_tgt) / [S_int(R_tgt) + S_int(R_ref)]` (relative to a reference report, range 0-1); `S_int = Σ_d W_d · S_d`.
- **Weights are NOT fixed defaults — they are inferred per task** (`W_d = (1/T) Σ w_d^(j)`, summing to 1). The one published illustrative case weighted **Insight 0.35, Comprehensiveness 0.30**, remainder to Instruction-Following + Readability. (This matches the pattern that Insight+Comprehensiveness dominate weighting on hard tasks.)
- **FACT framework**: Effective Citations (count of valid support relationships) + Citation Accuracy (precision of attributions).

**Agentic-RL-for-search line (survey arXiv:2506.18959 + primary papers):**
- **Iterative retrieval beats single retrieval**: "a single search step often falls short of capturing comprehensive information." Multi-hop iterative search+reason progressively builds context → drives Comprehensiveness.
- **Reasoning-search co-evolution loop**: retrieval and reasoning treated as interdependent and continuously refined — reasoning refines queries, retrieved info refines reasoning.
- **Test-time scaling**: allocate compute to reasoning depth (reasoning-heavy tasks → Insight) vs search breadth (search-heavy → Comprehensiveness).
- Named methods: **Search-R1** (open RL framework interleaving search+reasoning, veRL-based; Search-R1-32B); **R1-Searcher** (two-stage outcome-supervised RL, 7B); **DeepResearcher** (RL in real web environments); **WebThinker**; **ReSearch** (interpretable reasoning); ZeroSearch, WebAgent-R1.
- Repos: https://github.com/PeterGriffinJin/Search-R1 · https://arxiv.org/html/2503.05592 (R1-Searcher)

**WebThinker (arXiv:2504.21776, NeurIPS 2025) — most relevant to report writing:**
- **Autonomous Think-Search-and-Draft**: interleaves reasoning, retrieval, and **report drafting in real time** (not draft-at-end); plus a Deep Web Explorer for gap-triggered navigation. Trained with iterative online DPO.
- **Ablation is the key finding**: removing the **Autonomous Report Draft** capability caused the **largest performance drop** — continuous drafting, not post-hoc writing, is what drives report quality.
- On scientific report writing (Glaive), WebThinker scored **8.1 avg quality**, beating Gemini 2.0 Deep Research and Grok3 DeeperSearch. WebThinker-32B-RL hit SOTA among 32B models with gains up to **+21.5%**.

Sources: https://arxiv.org/pdf/2506.11763 · https://www.emergentmind.com/topics/race-reference-based-adaptive-criteria-evaluation · https://arxiv.org/html/2506.18959 · https://arxiv.org/abs/2504.21776

## 5. Techniques repeatedly cited as driving INSIGHT and COMPREHENSIVENESS

Consolidated across the sources, the recurring levers (with what each drives):

| Technique | Primarily drives | Evidence source |
|---|---|---|
| **Iterative / multi-hop retrieval** (vs single-shot) | Comprehensiveness | survey 2506.18959 |
| **Reasoning↔search co-evolution loop** (refine queries from intermediate deductions) | Insight + Comprehensiveness | survey 2506.18959 |
| **Gap-analysis / reflection loops** ("identify missing info & discrepancies") | Comprehensiveness | Gemini design; WebThinker Deep Web Explorer |
| **Planning / plan-first (approved outline)** | Instruction-Following + structure | Gemini planner; Anthropic lead-agent plan |
| **"Start wide then narrow" breadth control** | Comprehensiveness | Anthropic |
| **Parallel subagents / more sources** | Comprehensiveness | Anthropic (90.2% gain); Gemini 111 citations |
| **Test-time compute / token budget** | Both (≈80% of variance) | Anthropic |
| **Interleaved continuous drafting** (not draft-at-end) | Report quality overall | WebThinker ablation (largest drop) |
| **RL on real browsing tasks** (end-to-end) | Both | OpenAI; DeepResearcher; Search-R1 |
| **Larger / stronger generator model** | ~5% of variance (smaller lever) | Anthropic |
| **Cross-source contradiction resolution / synthesis (vs summarization)** | Insight | survey; Gemini "discrepancies" grounding |

**Bottom-line pattern relevant to RACE:** Insight and Comprehensiveness carry the highest RACE weights on hard tasks (illustrative 0.35 / 0.30), and the techniques that move them are iterative reason-search loops, gap-driven reflection, breadth-then-depth source expansion, and continuous drafting — while raw generator-model upgrade is a comparatively small lever (~5% of variance in Anthropic's decomposition, dominated by token budget at ~80%).

**Key source URLs:**
- OpenAI: https://openai.com/index/introducing-deep-research/
- Anthropic multi-agent: https://www.anthropic.com/engineering/built-multi-agent-research-system
- Gemini: https://gemini.google/overview/deep-research/ · https://www.philschmid.de/deep-research-update
- DeepResearch Bench (leaderboard + paper): https://deepresearch-bench.github.io/ · https://arxiv.org/pdf/2506.11763
- Agentic-search survey: https://arxiv.org/html/2506.18959
- WebThinker: https://arxiv.org/abs/2504.21776 · Search-R1: https://github.com/PeterGriffinJin/Search-R1 · R1-Searcher: https://arxiv.org/html/2503.05592

**Marked UNKNOWN (unpublished):** OpenAI DR exact source count / token budget; Gemini DR's exact underlying model version for the current 2.5-Pro DR, its 1M-context source-holding mechanism, and any RETRO/reciprocal synthesis internals (Google has not published these); fixed RACE dimension weights (there are none — they are inferred per task).
